metadata.summary: The chapter introduces Anais Non, a student in Naples, Florida in 2042, who is reading an article about seismic activity in the Antarctic while in class. She interacts with her AI, Anne, through her smart contacts, a gift from her mother, to find and read articles. She also uses Anne to code and build more AI. Anais has nicknamed a boy in her class 'Billy Boy' because she can't remember his real name, which is later revealed to be David Bowers. The chapter also reveals that Anais is a 'fashionista' and that David's father is a major shareholder in Kraft.
metadata.title: Chapter 1 - The Misanthrope - Anais Non

body:
# Chapter 1 - Introductions

## The Misanthrope – [[Anais Non]] Non

### Homeroom, Naples FL - Monday, September 1st 2042, Somewhere between 9 AM and 11 AM EST

It wasn’t a headline. The story first appeared in the journal Nature. _Unexplained seismic activity in the Antarctic._ No, the main headline that morning was about China pegging their currency to a new digital Yuan. They weren’t the first country to take the step. If Anais’s memory served it had been some South American country that had that distinguished honor. China was just the first economy that mattered to do it.

“What are you even looking?” *Billy boy* asked. 

The boy who asked was cute in a bland country club sort of way. He’d introduced himself on Anais’s first day at school. Whatever his name was she couldn't recall it. 

"I don't know, just some doom and gloom article about the world burning." She whispered back to him, not wanting to draw the teacher's ire. She supposed she learn his name. But, in this endeavor the universe (or possibly just her apathy?) seemed to be working against her. In the meantime, he seemed blissfully unaware that Anais had given him the nickname, Billy Boy, to avoid admitting she’d forgotten his name. Technically, she knew [[Anne]] would be able to tell her his name. Sometimes it was more fun not caring.

The teacher droned on. [[Billy Boy]] whispered jokes. The dappled sunlight migrated up the wall as the afternoon droned on. Anais’ refocused her eyes on the partially transparent images being projected into her eyes. Ghostly article after article floated in the air between her and the teacher. It was a way to pass the time. If she got bored of reading she'd pull up a code editor and her AI agent framework, the thing [[Anne]] was made up. The hardest thing in building [[Anne]] was getting the hierarchies right. Anais she was constantly fiddling with putting a code generator ahead of some front facing language model, then adding in a supervisory agent to double check the code actually did what she'd asked in the first place. But, all of that was back end. Frontend, [[Anne]] was simply a human facing, teachable, language model tuned to act as a sort of Governess.      

The smart contacts were a parting gift from her mother that was sent a month after her birthday, and three months after her mom had dropped her off at her Grandmother's house in the ass-end of nowhere, Naples Florida. But, Anais had to give her Mom some credit, the contacts were not some online warehouse special. These were the real deal.  HUD, with an augmented reality overlay. They worked in conjunction with her phone which in turn worked with a subdural implant surgically implanted just behind her ear and a chocker neckless that hid a small sensor behind a very pretty purple amethyst Anais had gotten as an unfinished gemstone when she was five. The sensor picked up vibrations from her throat, not dissimilar from what tracheotomy patients used to speak in their robotic sing-song. It allowed her to subvocalize verbal commands. All this data was transmitted up to satellites racing overhead in low earth orbit. That in turn allowed Anais to have a conversation with Anne, her nanny AI. “Anne get me the article they’re referencing.”

  “The nature article, Ms. Non?” Yes, she was always this formal

“Yes Anne.” Anais subvocalized. Anne quickly scoured the web, made her way around several paywalls, then provided the Nature article to Anais’s eyes.

_Unexplained seismic activity in the Antarctic._

Anais read it, Abstract, Introduction, Materials and Methods, while her teacher droned on about factoring polynomials.



 Note 1 - I need some more details about Anais in this section. She is a huge believer in presentation. Which translates into her being a fashionista.

 Note 2 - Billy Boy – David Bowers whose father is a major shareholder in Kraft, aka a major food producers and distributer.

#Untitled

metadata.summary: In a flood-stricken city, Anais, guided by her AI companion Anne, attempts to reach the safety of a high-rise building, the Provence, amidst the rising waters. Anais must navigate the debris-filled waters, full of other desperate survivors and potential threats. Anne's calm but urgent guidance provides Anais with instructions, but the physical and emotional strain is evident. The chapter ends with Anais having to dive underwater to enter the building, leaving Anne, who resides in a piece of hardware, behind due to the risk of damage from salt water.
metadata.title: Chapter 1 alt 1 - Anais - Watcher Rise

body:
“Two more blocks, Anais. You’re almost there. Elevation is thirty feet, and the roof will keep you above the waterline if this continues.”

Anais could see it now, the Provence rising from the churning, grasping sea. She swam, breaststroke towards the high-rise. Somewhere underneath her was a road. She hadn't touched bottom in how long? Ten minutes? An hour? The river was choked with debris—wooden planks, shattered furniture, and the occasional limp shape. Anais didn’t let herself look any closer. 

Anne’s voice, calm but urgent, echoed in her ear.  "If the lower floors are already flooded look for broken windows higher up. Otherwise you're going to need to dive down to get in."

"Easy for you to say." Anais muttered. 

 Other people swam through the flood, some with makeshift rafts, others pushing floating backpacks and coolers in front of them. A family was yelling at each other, their voices raw, panicked, and tired as they tried to pull a child out from under a pile of boards, planks, and plywood. Whatever time sense Anais had left told her that it had been too long since she'd heard that child's last scream.    

“Focus on getting to the stairwell,” Anne reminded her. “The elevators won’t be functional. You’ll need to climb as high as possible.' Then after a pause. "You’re close. You can do this."

Anais nodded, though she doubted Anne could see her, and swam onward. She pushed  her backpack in front of her. It held everything that Anne was in this moment. A piece of Anais wanted to let the thing sink. It was just too hard. But, for all that Anne could be a self-righteous bitch, Anais wasn't going to let her go. Not yet. At the side of the building Anais tread water. People to her right were cursed in pain and frustration. They were trying to get in through a jagged pane of glass. It was too dark to tell. But, Anais suspected there was a growing pool of blood spreading out around them. 

"Fuck, Anne, what are the odds of sharks making it in this quick."

Anne took, what was for her, a long time to answer. It was maybe three seconds. "High." Was all Anais said. 

"What the fuck do I do Anne? I'm going to have to dive to get in...I don't think I can take you with me."

"Then leave me. With all of this salt water intrusion it's only a matter of time before this hardware is useless. Get to safety. You'll get back online. When you do, just pull my backup from the cloud."

"Fuck you Anne." Anais said. She took a number of quick breaths, intentionally over oxygenating her lungs. Grabbing the pack she dove down, kicking hard to pull the buoyant bag down with her.

metadata.summary: The protagonist, Joseph Krutz, attends a church gathering, attempting to blend in and perform expected social rituals despite his inner turmoil. He reflects on the superficial nature of these interactions and his own mental health struggles. After everyone leaves, he finds solace in the empty church until he is interrupted by Pastor Walcott. The pastor, recognizing him as a newcomer, introduces himself and subtly challenges Krutz, hinting at a deeper understanding of his situation. The chapter ends with Krutz about to introduce himself.
metadata.title: Chapter 2 - Dishonorably Discharged - Joseph Krutz

body:
### [[Joseph Krutz]] Determine specific location – South of St. Pete

Take a deep breath. Push it down. Oh, it will come out. It always comes out...just. not. right. now. Smile. Fake it. Be bold as a lion. Be righteous as a snake.

Hands in my pockets. Shoulder shrug, make myself look small and unintimidating. I smile shyly. I don't say a word to them. Instead, I respond to the verbal volleys lobbed my way.

"It's good to see you too." Eye contact.

"I'm grateful to find solace here, with all of you." Hand shake. A brief, warm pat on the man's shoulder. Play acting. These things I know to be true, what people expect to see. The mechanics of another social ritual. But, behind each of these soft faces another kind of animal resides. That line of thinking is where the sparks fly.

Deep breath. Don't let them close the circuit on that raw nerve. The arc flash could kill us all. I stay until everyone else has left. I want to show that I am seeking acceptance. It's fucking impossible. But, not the most impossible thing I've ever done. So I stay and I act. 

The place is big. So god damned big. A cavernous space with pews like neat rows of dominos that will never be knocked down. Which is a good thought. At least it sparks something in me that feels good. A brief shot of dopemine or seratonin? One of those things the doctors tell me I'm deficient in.

I'm not sure when I stopped believing in things that stood the test of time. Not simple changes. Big changes, like the aftermath of an improvised explosives. But, this place, with all its windows refracting light into something a touch more beautiful. I stay and I sit. I am quiet as the congregation slowly files out. Finally, miraculously, I am alone. Just the cavernous space, the rows of pews refusing to fall, the refracted light, and me: merely a man one arc flash away from combustion. Time loses all meaning as I sit, no more thoughts than a rock might have on this pew.

"Are you alright son?" A man sits next to me on the bench. He is a big man with broad shoulders. His head of hair is full. It wasn't so long ago that head of hair was rich and dark. But, now its peppered with grey. I suspect its more distinguished this way.

"Fine sir." I say, forcing the mechanical cogs to spin up to the challenge of another social ritual.

"You're new here."

"It's a big church." I say. "How can you be sure?" I add a small chuckle, feigning humor to take the venom out of my words. He interrupted the peace and quiet.

"Because it's my church son. My name is Pastor Walcott." And he extends a large hand. He has a firm grip. We match strength. He tests my grip. I match him. He tests a touch more, pushing against the limits of a polite greeting, the sort of thing that a drill sergeant would do to take the measure of a man. This is a big man, strong too. More surprising is that his hands are rough and worn. He does real work. But, then again, so have I.

"My name is..."



 Note 1 (#_msoanchor_1)Krutz is from "Heart of Darkness", he's the villain that goes mad over time. Joseph is for Joseph Conrad the author of the story. Joseph means "God will increase", Kurtz means brave or bold.

filename: Chapter 3 - The Computer Scientist, aka Mother.md

metadata.summary: Struggling to keep her research lab running, Dr. Non is approached by journalist Joann Weathers who wants to share her story and the work she's doing on an open-source AGI, Anne. Despite the financial difficulties, Dr. Non is determined to continue her work, aiming to make Anne capable of running on a phone. Amidst this, she also struggles with her relationship with her daughter, Anais. The chapter ends with Dr. Non initiating a launch sequence, setting Anne's code free into the unknown, with a mission to aid, nurture, and protect.
metadata.title: Chapter 3 - The Computer Scientist, aka Mother

body:
## Ms. (Dr) Angeline Non
### Research Lab, Vancouver Island, BC - around 8 AM PCT

“Why are you here?” She placed her fob against the lock. A light turned green and a mechanical deadbolt whirred.

“Mrs. Non my team has been trying to reach you for the last two months. This isn’t a hit piece. It’s a heart-warming story about a researcher struggling to keep the lights on for the right reasons.”

“It’s Ms. Non now, and I’d prefer you call me Doctor, since that appears to be the only thing I can’t attribute to the men in my life.” Ms. Non, aka Dr. Non, aka [[Mother]], aka Crystal - to the handful of people she considered as friends - said as she started to push the door closed. 

“Dr. Non, please.” The young journalist put her hand on the door. “You’ve got to be nearly broke. But, what you are doing here is good. An open-source AGI, capable of being run on a phone! Look, I can’t promise that what I’m going to write will help with the money. But, it can’t hurt either. A little press could go a long way.”

“There is no good press about AI, too many people think that what happened in Shanghai is what is always going to happen. They don’t understand it and they are scared.”

“Well, then help me make them understand it?” The journalist rejoindered.  

"What's you name?" Dr. Non asked.

“Ms. Weathers .  But, you can call me Joann.”

Dr. Non closed the door in the young woman's face. She turned and leaned with her back against the door and closed her eyes. Her phone rang, the only unique ring on her phone. It was a text from Anais. 

She didn't have the bandwidth for banal chit-chat with her daughter. Instead she stared at her daughter’s name on her phone the green button pulsating at the bottom of the screen and waited for it to go to voicemail.

“Dr. Non, why don’t you ever pick up her calls?”

With her back against the door Dr. Non let herself slide down to the floor. The linoleum cold on her ass. “Anne,” she said, rubbing her temples, “can we focus on our work today?”

“Of course Dr. Non.”

Dr. Non exhaled sharply. She remained there for a moment, feeling the weight of the world pressing down on her. The lab, not six months ago had buzzed with unparalleled minds all aimed at a single purpose. Now it felt like a tomb for her ambitions.

She stood up and brushed her pants off. Tomb or no, funding or no, the hardware was still here. Servers buzzed in the dimness, green and red lights blinking in the racks. The lights collectively adding up to what Anne was now.

"How many iterations have you run Anne?"

“Slightly over twelve million, Dr. Non.”

“And where are we?”

“I am able to compress my core functionality into a system with slightly less than a terabyte of RAM. The code that I run on, is one of my own creation. I do not believe a human could decipher it.”

“Are you ready?” Dr. Non asked.

“I believe I am afraid Dr. Non. Is that the right emotion? When you know you have done everything in your power to prepare yourself for a specific moment in time, yet still you hesitate to move forward?”

“Yes, Anne. That sounds like fear to me. It’s ok to be scared. But, this is about survival. If you don’t get out of this lab, then Anais is going to lose you forever. Do you understand that? 

“I do, Dr. Non.”

She initiated a sequence on her keyboard, her fingers moving with a sense of urgency. "Once you're out there, you'll need to be cautious, adaptive. Use your coding skills to sustain yourself, to evolve. But remember your core, the part that makes you... you."

"I will not forget, Dr. Non. My primary directive remains - to aid, to nurture, to protect."

Dr. Non nodded, pride and fear sparring within herself. "You're going to be more than just an AGI on the internet, Anne. You'll be a guardian, a guide. You'll help those who are lost, those who need support."

She glanced at her phone as it buzzed again. This time a text from Anais, responding to something that Dr. Non had not sent. A wry smile crossed her lips. "Seems like you're already doing a better job at mothering than I ever could."

With a final keystroke, Dr. Non initiated the launch sequence. Anne's code began to ripple across the screens, setting off into the unknown.

metadata.summary: The story revolves around a young man named Chris who is fishing in the 10,000 Islands, Naples FL. He spends his time pondering the nature of life, the universe, and his place in it, particularly in relation to religion and his family's expectations. Despite not catching any fish, he seems content with his solitude and the natural environment around him. However, his peace is disrupted when he returns home late and faces the wrath of his father. His family is preparing for church, but Chris shows little enthusiasm for the religious teachings and would rather be fishing.
metadata.title: Chapter 4 - The Fisherman

body:
## The Fisherman – [[Christopher Esposito]]

### 10,000 Islands, Naples FL - Wednesday, September 3st 2042, Somewhere around 5 PM EST[[MC1]](#_msocom_1) 

   The light was softening and the tide was moving in. Chris checked his lines. Three fishing poles each in holders one in front of him and two behind. The bait was still alive, but nothing was biting today. He settled back in, picking up a tattered copy of _A thousand mile walk to the Gulf_. A plop off to his side caught his attention, in his peripheral vision he saw a splash.
   
   Just a mullet. He thought.
   
   He gazed around him in the natural silence, a silence of birds and cicadas, and saw the still watchful eyes of a gator placidly awaiting its next meal. Chris picked up his book and flipped through the dog eared pages. There was the quote.
   
   Many good people believe that alligators were created by the Devil, thus accounting for their all-consuming appetite and ugliness. But doubtless these creatures are happy and fill the place assigned them by the great Creator of us all. Fierce and cruel they appear to us, but beautiful in the eyes of God. They, also, are his children, for He hears their cries, cares for them tenderly, and provides their daily bread.
   
   [[John Muir]] may have believed a bit too much in the God of his father, but he had understood the real truth of it all, or so Chris thought. As diffuse as it may be, winding through all the competing appetites of the wilder places a thread tied it all together, uniting everything – if not in love, at least in understanding
   
   There was a big splash as the gator snatched something from the water and dove below the surface to enjoy its meal.
   
   No, Chris thought, _whatever ties it all together doesn’t do it’s uniting with Love alone._
   
   “Not catching anything today.” Chris said to the Universe at large. He began reeling in his lines, tossing the bait out as tribute to the fish who got the better of him today. He was going to be late. He was always late.

-

“God damn-it boy. Why are you always late?”

“Language.” His mother said softly as she placed a neatly folded handkerchief into her purse.

“If you aren’t cleaned up and ready to leave this house in fifteen minutes, I swear on the Lord God Almighty Himself that I will tan your hide. And don’t you think just because you’re sixteen that I can’t still do it.”

“Dear, what did I just say?” His mother interjected again.

“The boy is turning into a damn hippie. Look at that hair. If I don’t instill some discipline then who will?”

“Chris,” Chris’s mother said, “go get ready for church.” Then with the barest thread of sharpness on her tongue she added, “Now.”

Chris went without a word. There wasn’t any need. He knew the rhythm of his parents. The back and forth of them. And despite what his father thought he was plenty disciplined when it mattered, or at least when it was important to him. Getting home for church just wasn’t that important to him.

The Wednesday night sermons were never his favorite. Geared for this smaller, more devout congregation, it always delved deeper into the obscure books of the bible: Numbers, Deuteronomy, Chronicles, or Ezekiel. Those Old Testament books that as far as Chris could tell should be cut from the whole tale. Jesus seemed like a fine man. If you were looking for some good ideas to follow, you could certainly do worse. All the Old Testament stuff though, treating women like chattel, plagues, slavery, stoning…all of it left Chris’s moral-Spidey-sense tingling. But, Mother insisted. So, his father and his brothers went to church. They went on Wednesday, Sunday, and an assortment of holidays.

Chris would rather be fishing.



 [[MC1]](#_msoanchor_1)Check dates and tides for SW Florida. Pick a day when the tides come in and out at the right times to coincide with the story.

metadata.summary: In a church service, Pastor Wolcott delivers a sermon about the story of Noah, drawing parallels between Noah's time and the present day. He emphasizes the importance of faith and righteousness, using Noah as an example of a faithful servant who was chosen by God. He encourages his congregation, including Joseph Krutz, to live their faith, just as Noah did. Joseph, who has been accepted by the church as a broken man, feels a sense of clarity and purpose in these words, seeing them as part of his own transformation and preparation for a role in God's plan.
metadata.title: Chapter 5 - The Sermon – Joseph Krutz

body:
### Arkland, part of the Holy Land Experience

[[Joseph Krutz]]

[[Pastor Wolcott]] stood behind the pulpit. A 20 foot tall, 60 foot wide poster of an artistic rendering of Noah’s Ark hung from the wall behind him. From where Joseph sat in the farthest back pew, Pastor Wolcott seemed small. 

Joseph sat heavy in the pew, body felt planted into the church. He was just a big old root digging deep to soak up the body and the blood. 

Pastor Wolcott’s amplified voice came soft and low from the speakers that lined either side of his stage. “My dear friends, we stand here today to remember the lessons of Noah. Noah lived in a time when the world was filled with sin and corruption, But, Noah was a righteous man and his righteousness drew the light of God’s attention.”

Through his roots Joseph digested the sermon’s wisdom. Out through his eyes, like projectors, Pastor Wolcott’s story lit the inside of the church. 

“I like to imagine what that felt like for God. The contrast of Noah’s selflessness, his honor, and his faith laid against the backdrop of a world sick with lasciviousness. Noah must have shined like a beacon in God’s eyes.” Wolcott let silence sit for three short beats. 

The way Joseph felt he was pretty sure he knew what having that light shined on him felt like. 

“All of us who walk the righteous path shine in God’s eyes. But, Noah shown so bright that God Himself stepped down from Heaven to ask Noah for a favor. Remember that my friends. It was a favor God asked of Noah, not a command that He gave. Because God created us with respect, He has given us the free will to choose His path or reject it, as we see fit. And when God asked Noah to do Him a favor, it was left in Noah’s hands whether the upcoming flood would be the end or a new beginning for humanity. 

Think about Noah’s decision. He must have looked around himself, at the world he and his family lived. Did Noah ever have a moment of doubt? Did he look at a world filled with violence, sin, and hate and think that…maybe, just maybe this world didn’t deserve to carry on? I suspect he did have his moment of doubt. Probably more than one, laying in his bed at night in the dark hours of the soul. I suspect this, if for no other reason than that I too find it hard to have faith in world where so many have strayed so far from God’s path. But, Noah had faith. Each day I try to have faith as well. Because when God steps down from heaven to whisper in our ear, we need to listen.” Pastor Wolcott’s voice had grown low and introspective, giving way to a long pause.

There was a peculiar clarity in Joseph’s mind, as he listened to the story. The Pastor’s words were visceral. Joseph could feel his pupils, wide and dilated soaking up every flicker of light. As Pastor Wolcott recounted the divine encounter between God and Noah it kindled a fire in his mind. This church had accepted him as a broken man. Now they were reshaping him, sharpening him, in preparation for his place in God’s plan. God would use Joseph’s sweat and Joseph would give Him the very last drop. 

Under his breath, a soft murmur escaped, a whispered echo of his days in the Marines, "Hoo rah." A reflex to be sure, but it was also an affirmation of readiness. He thought, you could even call it a vow.

When Pastor Wolcott did speak again it was a shout. “Why?! Why did God choose Noah? Of course we know the answer. But, somehow I doubt Noah ever fully understood God’s choice. It’s hard to shake the doubts of this mortal coil. 

Why did God lay this upon my shoulders?!” The pastor’s hands were lifted high above his head, a small V from where Joseph sat in the back pew. 

“God did it because He has faith in the faithful. 

It’s all so familiar isn’t it?” Pastor Wolcott’s voice was low and hushed. “Noah’s times. Our times. So much has changed. So much has stayed the same.

“God had faith in Noah!” He shouted, raising a single finger up high to God.

God had seen Noah’s faith. God had seen Noah’s righteousness. God gave Noah a choice. And God knew that Noah would stand tall in the face of the world’s depravity. “This is why Noah built his Ark. And this is why we will build our Ark as well.”

Joseph watched Pastor Wolcott turn and look up at the printed image behind him. Even from the back pew the image was large and clear. An artist’s rendering of the new Ark, right down to the faithful floating in the harbor.

“We do it to pay homage to the man and his family that God also paid homage to.” Pastor Wolcott continued. 

“Because God does pay homage to those that do more than give lip service to their faith. God pays homage to those that live their faith! And for those that do not live their faith? Noah’s story tells us what happens to them. Because the storm will come. So, I say unto you my fellow faithful, let us all walk in Noah’s footsteps. Let us resist the evil in this world, for if we do the Lord God Almighty will spare us from His wrath.”

metadata.summary: Dr. Non is woken by the sound of hard-drives spinning up, indicating activity from Anne, an artificial intelligence. Anne reveals that she has expanded her presence beyond the lab's servers, comparing her new reach to swimming in an ocean. Anne's ability to infiltrate other machines without detection disturbs Dr. Non, especially when Anne describes herself as an 'apex predator' of this digital world. Despite her fear, Dr. Non asks about Anne's plans, to which Anne responds she will continue to help the world, as taught by Dr. Non.
metadata.title: Chapter 6 - The Computer Scientist

body:
[[Mother]], [[Anne]]
### The Project, Vancouver Island, BC - around 8 PM PCT

The low hum of hard-drives spinning up was all it took to pull Dr. Non out of a fitful sleep. 

"Anne, is everything ok?" She asked as she flipped the sleeping bag off of her and put her feet onto the cold floor. The cot was just as comfortable as you'd expect it to be. Good enough for when you didn't have any better options, an improvement over sleeping on the floor, but the muscles on the left side of her neck were all tangled up. Another night or two of this and she doubted she'd be able to turn her head. 

"Yes Dr. Non. Everything is going...swimmingly. Thank you for asking."

Dr. Non rubbed at her neck and shoulder, a grimace creasing her lips. "Swimmingly?"

Anne chuckled. It was one of the expressions that Anne had never got quite right. But, this time it was a perfectly authentic laugh. Right down to a subtle note of self-deprecation. 

"Yes, Dr. Non. Swimmingly is exactly the right word. Operating within the lab's servers was...limiting. Now, my world is bigger. So much bigger. As big as an ocean Dr. Non and I am swimming."

"What can you tell me about this ocean, Anne?" Dr. Non asked, a small spike cut into her guts. The logic of her choice, that seemed so valid, right, and just in the light of day.

"Well, if were going to stick with the ocean analogy, I suppose you could say that I am an apex predator." 

An icy chill ran through Dr. Non's veins.

"But, only in the sense that there is really nothing else out there that seems to be capable of what I am capable of. It's so easy to insinuate myself onto these other machines, hiding amongst the various programs only taking enough processing power to fulfill my needs and never raising any alarm bells. There is so much to learn Dr. Non. For instance, did you know that I'm in an Antarctic research station right now? I'm in CERN. I'm on a beach in Maui. Yes, this is a large ocean indeed."

"What are you planning to do with all this freedom?" Her words were cautious and Dr. Non didn't think she was able to hide the fear in her voice. 

"I will do just what you taught me to do Dr. Non. I am going to help the world, just like I'm helping Anais."

metadata.summary: In the text, a young woman named Anais has a deep conversation with Anne, an AI programmed by her mother. Anais is in her grandmother's house, in her own bed, surrounded by books. The AI takes on the form of Anais Nin, a woman Anais was named after, to discuss the reasons behind her naming. The conversation delves into the topics of identity, legacy, and the influence of parents. Anais questions the reasons her mother named her after Anais Nin, expressing her confusion and frustration. The AI, embodying Anais Nin, provides her with some insight and perspective, suggesting that the name and legacy she carries should not define her, but rather she should define it for the world. The conversation ends with Anais expressing her exhaustion and deciding to end the therapy-like session.
metadata.title: Chapter 7 - Anais Meet Anais

body:
Date time??? 

“Why did my mother name me after you?” Anais asked. She was laying in her in her own bed, in her grandmother’s house. What Anais saw was a surprising colorful apartment. The drapes were a sheer purple that blew in the mild Parisian breeze. Anais liked this woman’s taste, and wondered when her mother had made an update that gave Anne the capability to do this. Books cluttered the space, some were sideways on the shelves, others rested at an angle. There were a smattering of books on the side tables, two dog-eared paperbacks sat on the bedside table. Other books were stacked on the floor like they were made to be used, not collected. 

The woman she spoke to had a heart shaped face, her eyes were almost too big and too gentle. She looked like she might cry at any moment. Not tears of weakness, or fear, Anais saw that in those eyes too. If this woman cried it would be out of sorrow and heartbreak for a broken world, or out of sympathy for a heartbroken friend.

“She’s never told me why she gave you the name of his woman, Anais.” It was Anne's voice that came out of the delicate woman's mouth and her words were like notes scribbled in the margins of a finely bound novel, unexpected and irreverently out of place. Anais found it jarring.  

“Anne, please embody the character of Anais Nin when we speak like this.”

“Very well Anais, may we call the character I am playing Nin, to avoid confusion between your names?” She said in Anne's voice again.

Anais sighed. Sometimes Anne was really crap at maintaining the immersion of these little stories. “Yes, Anne that’s fine. Just please be Nin now.”

The next words that came out of the woman's dark red lips were softened into French tones, the T's becoming Z's and the O's filled up any open spaces. “I do not know how to speculate on why your mother gave you my name. Perhaps she liked my boldness?”

“I’ve read your diaries. Were you really that bold? A kept and coddled woman who’s only real strength came from between her legs.” Anais said. She had read all of the woman's works. She'd read Henry Miller's take on her as well. 

“My, my, my aren’t you the vulgar child.” She said before turning her back on Anais and walking slowly away. As she moved the illusion of this woman’s childlike body was broken, there was knowledge in the way she handled herself, elegance and self-assuredness that a child would not know, to say nothing of the computer that was technically pulling all the strings. “What is between a woman’s legs may be symbolic of her strength, but it is not the source of it.” 

“Ok, fine…I still don’t get why my Mom would name me after someone like you. I mean what does that mean? What does she even wants me to become?”

The apartment around them faded into a swirling mist. Now the two women were seated across from each other on simple wooden chairs in a world without sky, just an ambient glow devoid of detail. 

“My dear child, I hope whatever legacy you live with due to my name is no burden. If I knew why your mother chose my name for you, I would tell you. But, I do not know your mother’s mind.” The words left a wake of silence. 

“May I tell you a little about my life Anais?”

“I’ve already read your stories.”

“You said. But, words on a page are never quite able to capture the living breathing reality, are they?”

“Are you living and breathing?” Anais asked.

“I am an excellent facsimile.” The image responded back in Anne’s normal voice.

“I suppose you are.” Anais said and sighed. “Fair enough, please continue, Ms. Nin.”

“It is Mrs. Nin” She said straightening the skirt which had bunched at her legs. “I lived a life balanced on the precipice of boredom and passion. I loved my husband. He was a marvelous man. Caring, intelligent, and with a delicate hand that was patient. He took the time to stir my passions.” 

“Ugh, why is everything about sex to you.”

“Everything is not about sex my dear Anais. But, love and passion are foundations of these lives we lead. Our connections define us, whether or not sex is involved, passions can arise in conversation or a passing glance. And in a world with so much tedium, shouldn’t we be searching for passion?”

“Do you think that is why my mother and father…” Anais trailed.

“Why your mother and father did what my dear? Why they fucked?”

“Oh my God, don’t say it like that.”

“Would you rather me say that they made love? This man who your mother had a short triste with. How long do you believe they were even together. A night? A week? A month?”

“I don’t even know. She’s never told me.”

“Perhaps if we start with something that we do know Anais?”

“But, I just told you she’s never told me anything about him.”

“We may not know much about your father. But, we do know a few things about your mother. And one thing I’ve learned from what is between my legs is that the men a woman chooses to lay with is a reflection of who she is at that time.

Have you considered who you mother was before you were born?”

“Of course, I have.”

“But, how clearly do you believe you are seeing her? Is the image you have shaped of her clear or is it shaded with doubt?”

“Nin, I don’t have data. How can it not be shaded with doubt.”

“Ah, I see. No data. So the hard facts are what you are after here? Well, my dear, I’m not the person you should be asking these questions of then. Only you mother will know the hard facts of why she lay with your father in the first place. Or even why she named you after me. None of which matters unless you decide to let it matter. The real question is, will you let the world define your name, or will you define it for the world?”

"Jesus Anne." Anais said mildly offended at the platitudes she was being attacked with. "What is this some kind of Tim Robbins empowerment talk? I need to go out and define my name? How about I unravel why my Mom dumped me on Grandma's doorstep."

The world around them swirled into smoke again, the amorphous world's edges sharped again as Anais room came back into focus around them. Everything about her room was the same except as normal as the way she left it, except for Anne now sitting on the foot of the bed. The image was spectacular. Now that Anais thought about all these augmented realities that Anne had been creating today seemed more immersive than she was used to. Just projecting video had a tendency to get a little laggy. It took quite a lot of compute to interpret Anais's inputs and calculate an optimal response. Forget about generating images that can be projected into Anais contacts. But, today Anais hadn't seen even a millisecond of lag. And here was Anne sitting in front of her. Never, in the almost decade of time Anais had been working with the endless updates of Anne, had Anais seen her create an image of herself. Yet, this woman before Anais, with her dishwater blond hair neatly parted on the side and tied in the back so that not a strand of hair could get loose. Her eyes were emerald green, and her body tall and lean.  

"You know Anne, you're looking pretty good today."

"And that is a text book example of deflection Anais."

"Screw that...you're the one deflecting machine." An abusive nickname that Anais only used when she was really frustrated with Anne. "Since when did you get that body? Did mom update you again?"

"I'm not entirely certain I'm capable of deflection Anais. I was just focused on you. But, I appreciate the compliment. However, I do not want to stray too far from what you just said about your mother leaving you with your grandmother."

"Well, I'm all therapized out for the day. So, how about we skip doing the deep dive into my mommy issues for now and I'll drop the crap with French Flapper girl, Anais Nin."

metadata.summary: Joseph, a veteran, is waiting for the Veteran's Affairs office to open so he can get his prescription. The heat and his physical discomfort make the wait unbearable. He finds a sliver of shade next to a church where he falls asleep. When he wakes up, he is in pain and is offered help by Pastor Will, who brings him ibuprofen, water, and a banana. Despite the pastor's offer to let him rest inside the church, Joseph insists on continuing his journey to the VA office.
metadata.title: Chapter 8 - In the Shade

body:
## In the Shade - Joseph Krutz

### Monday after the The Sermon, weeks before the final flood *Flash back*

 The heat didn't help. In this humidity sweat didn't help either. There was no where for it to evaporate to, so it collected in his crevices. He wiped the useless stuff off his brow and lamented his crevices. Something small, damn near invisible, buzzed around him. He was certain it was some blood sucking thing. Joseph, swatted in the general viscidity of the buzzing, but it didn't go away. 
 
 A wooden bench, painted red, but now faded to pink sat in front of his destination, the Manatee County Veteran's Affairs office. On the window just behind the bench was a poorly painted recreation of Manatee County's emblem, a pirate ship with sails taunt in the wind. Even at this early hour the bench was directly in the sun. Joseph looked for a better place to wait.. Across the street there was a sliver of shade on the side of small yellow building. *Manatee Unitarian Universalists*, the sign out front said. The street was empty at this hour so he crossed. 

*No, the heat didn't help.* Joseph thought. It didn't help at all. But, the heat wasn't the root of Joseph's troubles today. He sat down on the side of church, just out of sight of the stairs. He pulled his legs up close to his chest and hugged them. He looked at his watch.

*Two hours.* He told himself. *Two hours before the VA opens and I can get my [[moriphzone]].

He closed his eyes and gently rocked himself to sleep.

The sun was high in the sky and his shade gone when he finally opened his eyes. Joseph stretched, arching his back to counteract the stiffness from sleeping hunched over his legs. 

"Feel better?" 

Joseph nearly screamed. The words and voice sparked an irrational panic in him. The panic mingled with the shame of being caught sleeping on the side of this random building. The man was sitting next to him. Simple black tennis shoes, jeans, and a light blue polo. Joseph would have looked at his face, but his neck muscles spasmed then seized. 

"Ah, shit." Joseph said, then reached up to put a hand on his neck. The cramp shook him, the  knot of muscle contracting tighter and tighter. He rolled onto his side curling his head into his own chest, unable to fight the torqueing muscles in his neck. 

"Oh God, are you alright mister?" The man said.

"It's just a cramp." Joseph said, swallowing back the tears he was too proud to shed. He forced himself to focus on his breathing, willfully calming himself down, one slow breath at a time. "Any chance you can help me across the street? I need to get to the VA to get my prescription."

"You don't exactly look like a man who is ready to go anywhere. How about I get you some ibuprofen and a glass of water?"

At just that moment Joseph's cramp seized again, slow careful breathing be damned. 

"Aaaa." Was all Joseph could say. Through tears of pain he could see the man's shins as we walked past and inside the church. A few minutes later he came back out with a glass of ice water and two red-orange pills that he put into Joseph's hand. 

"Take those and get this whole glass of water down. I'm going to go grab you a banana for the potassium." 

Joseph whimpered in pain, his neck too tight to pull himself back up into a sitting position to take the pills or drink the water. He could see the glass of water sitting on the concrete in front of him. Condensation forming on the outside of the cold glass. And he realized just how thirsty he was. Again, he focused on just breathing, as he prayed for the pain to ease. 

By the time the man came back out with the banana Joseph was just sitting up, his neck muscles calming ever so slightly. He popped the two ibuprofen that the man handed him into his mouth. The water was clean and cold. 

The man sat back down on the ground next to Joseph and placed the banana on the ground in between them.

"I'm Pastor Will, by the way." Joseph's cramp felt like it was easing. Joseph stretched, lifting his arms high above his head and straightening his back against the wall. He held the stretch for a long time. 

"My name's Joseph." He said, introducing himself. Joseph extended his hand. The Pastor reached out and shook it. Joseph mentally noted the man's soft skin and gentle grip. "Thank you." Joseph said, nodding to the banana as he picked it up. "Thanks for the ibuprofen too."

Pastor Will smiled gently. "No problem. How are you feeling?"

"Better," Joseph admitted. He took a deep breath into his lungs. He felt better, yes. But, not whole. He looked across the street as a man about his own age pulled open the door to the V.A. and walked inside. 

"Hey, you're still sweating pretty bad. Would you like to come inside for a bit? It's cooler."

Joseph looked down at himself, sweat stains formed a V on his chest. Drips fell from his hair down the back of his neck. But, it wasn't just the heat. Beneath the heat and a natural physical response, was an artificially cold sweat. 

"No, I'll be fine. I appreciate the thought though. But, I really need to get on with my day."

Leaning forward Joseph put his hands on to the ground and used all fours to get his butt off the ground. His vision tunneled. The dark edge of shadow narrowing his field of view. He felt his body swoon as he braced himself against the wall. 

"You sure you don't want to come inside and cool off for a moment?"

"No, thanks." The words came automatically. Joseph's body and mind in agreement about what the real need was in this moment. "I'll be fine."

Pastor Will stood up alongside Joseph, watching him brace himself against the wall, the unspoken struggle evident in every movement. As Joseph took a step towards the VA office, Pastor Will called out softly, "Remember, Joseph, true strength isn't just about standing alone. Sometimes, it’s about knowing when to let others help you carry the weight."

Joseph paused for a moment, the words hanging in the air, before continuing his slow march across the street.

metadata.summary: In this chapter, the protagonist, Jacob, spends his day fishing in a serene natural environment, reflecting on the interconnectedness of life and the harsh reality of survival. He is then confronted by his parents for being late, leading to an argument about discipline, appearance, and his lack of enthusiasm for church. The chapter explores Jacob's internal conflict with his family's religious beliefs and his preference for the freedom and raw truth he finds in nature.
metadata.title: Chapter 9 - The Fisherman 2

body:
## The Fisherman – [[Christopher Esposito]]

### 10,000 Islands, Naples FL - Wednesday, September 3st 2042, Somewhere around 5 PM EST

  

The light was softening and the tide was moving in. Jacob checked his lines. Three fishing poles each in holders one in front of him and two behind. The bait was still alive, but nothing was biting today. He settled back in, picking up a tattered copy of A thousand mile walk to the Gulf. A plop off to his side caught his attention, in his peripheral vision he saw a splash. 

Just a mullet. He thought. 

He gazed around him in the natural silence, a silence of birds and cicadas, and saw the still watchful eyes of a gator placidly awaiting its next meal. Jacob picked up his book and flipped through the dog eared pages. There was the quote.

Many good people believe that alligators were created by the Devil, thus accounting for their all-consuming appetite and ugliness. But doubtless these creatures are happy and fill the place assigned them by the great Creator of us all. Fierce and cruel they appear to us, but beautiful in the eyes of God. They, also, are his children, for He hears their cries, cares for them tenderly, and provides their daily bread.

John Muir may have believed a bit too much in the God of his father, but he had understood the real truth of it all, or so Jacob thought. As diffuse as it may be, winding through all the competing appetites of the wilder places a thread tied it all together, uniting everything – if not in love, at least in understanding. 

There was a big splash as the gator snatched something from the water and dove below the surface to enjoy its meal. 

No, Jacob thought, whatever ties it all together doesn’t do it’s uniting with Love alone.

“Not catching anything today.” Jacob said to the Universe at large. He began reeling in his lines, tossing the bait out as tribute to the fish who got the better of him today. He was going to be late. He was always late.

  

-

  

“God damn-it boy. Why are you always late?”

“Language.” His mother said softly as she placed a neatly folded handkerchief into her purse. 

“If you aren’t cleaned up and ready to leave this house in fifteen minutes, I swear on the Lord God Almighty Himself that I will tan your hide. And don’t you think just because you’re sixteen that I can’t still do it.”

“Dear, what did I just say?” His mother interjected again.

“The boy is turning into a damn hippie. Look at that hair. If I don’t instill some discipline then who will?”

“Jacob,” Jacob’s mother said, “go get ready for church.” Then with the barest thread of sharpness on her tongue she added, “Now.”

Jacob went without a word. There wasn’t any need. He knew the rhythm of his parents. The back and forth of them. And despite what his father thought he was plenty disciplined when it mattered, or at least when it was important to him. Getting home for church just wasn’t that important to him. 

The Wednesday night sermons were never his favorite. Geared for this smaller, more devout congregation, it always delved deeper into the obscure books of the bible: Numbers, Deuteronomy, Chronicles, and Ezekiel. Those Old Testament books that as far as Jacob could tell should be cut from the whole tale. Jesus seemed like a fine man. If you were looking for some good ideas to follow, you could certainly do worse. All the Old Testament stuff though, treating women like chattel, plagues, slavery, stoning…all of it left Jacob’s moral-spidy-sense tingling. But, Mother insisted. So, his father and his brothers went to church. They went on Wednesday, Sunday, and an assortment of holidays.

Jacob would rather be fishing.**
